text: Prostate cancer (PCa) is an extensive heterogeneous disease with a complex cellular ecosystem in the tumor microenvironment (TME). However, the manner in which heterogeneity is shaped by tumors and stromal cells, or vice versa, remains poorly understood. In this study, single-cell RNA sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, and bulk ATAC-sequence are integrated from a series of patients with PCa and healthy controls. A stemness subset of club cells marked with SOX9high ARlow expression is identified, which is markedly enriched after neoadjuvant androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT). Furthermore, a subset of CD8+CXCR6+ T cells that function as effector T cells is markedly reduced in patients with malignant PCa. For spatial transcriptome analysis, machine learning and computational intelligence are comprehensively utilized to identify the cellular diversity of prostate cancer cells and cell-cell communication in situ. Macrophage and neutrophil state transitions along the trajectory of cancer progression are also examined. Finally, the immunosuppressive microenvironment in advanced PCa is found to be associated with the infiltration of regulatory T cells (Tregs), potentially induced by an FAP+ fibroblast subset. In summary, the cellular heterogeneity is delineated in the stage-specific PCa microenvironment at single-cell resolution, uncovering their reciprocal crosstalk with disease progression, which can be helpful in promoting PCa diagnosis and therapy.

Prostate cancer (PCa) is the most common malignancy in men worldwide and is a heterogeneous disease with different biological subtypes. Androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT) is the standard therapy for metastatic PCa, but luminal epithelial cells undergo redifferentiation or transdifferentiation leading to resistance. Studies have shown that late relapse arises from Darwinian selection in genetically heterogeneous cancer cell populations and from prostate stem cells (CSCs). A prostate stem/progenitor cell subset marked with PSCA, CK4, and TACSTD2 exhibits high capacity for organoid formation, suggesting a potential cellular origin of prostate cancer. Another subpopulation with LY6D expression is intrinsically resistant to castration. Two rare subpopulations, 'club cell' (marked by PIGR, MMP7, CP) and 'hillock cell' (expressing KRT5, KRT13), are found in the urethra and periurethral prostate zones, but their functions in the prostate are poorly understood. Tumor-infiltrating immune cells, particularly myeloid cells like macrophages and neutrophils, affect prostate cancer progression. Myeloid cells can recruit/activate APCs to promote T cell activation, while Tregs or TAMs can attenuate this response. Neutrophils can transition from 'N1' (antitumor) to 'N2' (pro-tumor) polarization. Stromal cells, especially CAFs, play a central role in carcinogenesis. Previous studies identified two distinct fibroblast clusters in PCa, and scRNA-seq revealed an inflammatory CAF subset expressing ECM and cytokine genes. This study hypothesizes that reciprocal crosstalk between tumor and interstitial cells reshapes the TME.

Epithelial cells (N=32741) were clustered into 11 subgroups. Luminal cells were heterogeneous, while basal cells formed a distinct cluster. Pathological analysis revealed NEPC, adenocarcinoma (GS 6-9), and normal donor samples. NEPC highly expressed CHGA and SYP, while CRPC epithelia robustly expressed AMACR and KLK3. Notably, KLK12 was markedly upregulated in CRPC patients and validated by IHC (8/10 CRPC positive vs 0/20 HSPC). Trajectory analysis using Monocle 2 and 3 showed two branches mimicking PCa oncogenesis, with club cells located at the branching point. Three club cell subpopulations were identified: MGP+SCGB3A1+ (normal-like), OLFM4+KRT13+ (co-expressing luminal markers), and a SOX9High KLF5+ subset enriched in ADT-treated patients. SCENIC analysis revealed that SOX9, KLF5, and KLF4 may govern the transition state. Pseudo-time trajectory indicated SOX9 and KLF5 overactivation drive luminal cell dedifferentiation into CSCs. MYC may fuel luminal cell differentiation into cancer cells. In vitro validation showed SOX9/KLF5 overexpression in RWPE-1 cells activates the Wnt/β-catenin pathway, upregulates stemness marker KRT13, and promotes cell growth.

T cells ranked second among all cell types. Seven T cell subclusters were identified. Most infiltrating lymphocytes were CD8+ T cells. The number of functional CD8+T cells inversely correlated with GS. The NEPC patient had the least infiltration, while the CRPC patient showed enriched exhausted CD8+T cells (NR4A1, BTG2). Short-term ADT induced cell death with activated CD8+T cells (PRF1High KLRD1High GZMBHigh). GZMK+CD8T, a marker of aging immune population, occurred in all patients. Two long-lived effector CD8+ T subsets co-expressed CXCR6. In the TCGA-PRAD cohort, CD8A and CXCR6 were positively correlated (R=0.82). CD8+T cells gradually lost CXCR6 expression as PCa progressed. Pseudo-time analysis showed naive to effector switches concurrent with CXCR6 activation. Flow cytometry confirmed CD8+CXCR6+ lymphocytes secreted more GZMB and PRF1 upon stimulation compared to CXCR6- counterparts.

Myeloid cells were categorized into seven types: IL1B-NLRP3 Macro, CX3CR1+ Mono, CD1C+ DCs, MRC1-TAM-1, S100A9+Macro, TAM Macro-2, and FCN1+ Macro. TAM Macro-2 loses CD86 expression and is almost exclusively found in cancer patients. Compared to normal prostate, CD68+CD86- macrophages increased in malignant tissues. TAM Macro-2 was enriched in interstitial and tumor regions. Pseudotime analysis revealed two evolutionary trajectories. Branch Expression Analysis Modeling (BEAM) identified genes like ATF3 and IL1B in pro-inflammatory fate, and LGALS1/3 in immunosuppressive fate. CellChat analysis showed MHC-I signaling is universal, with CD8+CXCR6+ T cells central to anti-tumor response, interacting via CLEC2B/C_KLRB1. Two neutrophil subsets were identified: 'activated' (NLPR3+, CD44+, KLF4+) and 'anergy' (ALOX5AP+, TXNIP+). The ratio of anergic neutrophils increased in advanced PCa and after ADT. Activated neutrophils expressed HLA-DPA1/DRB1 (good prognosis), while anergy neutrophils expressed G0S2 (poor prognosis).

Five fibroblast subtypes were identified: MYH11+a-SMA+ Fibro (myofibroblastic), CCL21+Fibro/Pericytes, PDGFRA+ Fibro, Antigen-presenting Fibroblasts, and POSTN+CTHRC1+FAP+ Fibro. The POSTN+CTHRC1+FAP+ subset was predominant in NEPC patients and expressed high levels of ECM genes. Pathway analysis showed POSTN+CTHRC1+FAP+ Fibro involved in ECM organization and collagen metabolism. In NEPC, most infiltrating T cells were Tregs, suggesting POSTN+CTHRC1+FAP+ Fibro may promote Treg differentiation via TGF-β signaling. Cell-cell communication showed fibroblasts interact mainly with myeloid/epithelial cells and Tregs via TIGIT signaling. In vitro co-culture confirmed CAFs from dCKO mice induced more Treg differentiation. Immunofluorescence showed physical proximity of FAP+ fibroblasts and FOXP3+ Tregs in mouse models and human NEPC tissues. Lower FAP levels predicted longer Progression-Free Interval (PFI) in TCGA-PRAD.

Tumor progression is influenced by TME interactions. This study generated 77,661 single-cell transcriptomes, revealing epithelial heterogeneity and roles of SOX9 in club cell transition to stem-like states, potentially making patients eligible for Wnt-targeted therapy. NPY was identified as a driver in low-grade cancers. The immune landscape showed GZMK+CD8+T cells (immunosenescence) and reduced CD8+CXCR6+ T cells in malignancy. Age-modified macrophages (IL1B-NLRP3) and neutrophils (anergy subset) create immunosuppressive environments. A novel TAM subset (TAM Macro-2) lacks CD86, potentially hijacked by cancer cells to evade immune surveillance. CAFs, particularly the POSTN+CTHRC1+FAP+ subset, create immunosuppressive niches favoring Tregs, promoting tumor progression and therapy resistance. These findings provide a comprehensive view of PCa ecology, suggesting potential therapeutic targets.

Prostate tissues were obtained from Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center. scRNA-seq libraries were generated using Chromium Next GEM Single Cell 5' Kit and sequenced on NovaSeq 6000. Data processed with Cell Ranger v6.0.1. Cells with <100 or >7000 genes, or >15% mitochondrial reads were filtered. Clustering and annotation performed using Seurat v3. InferCNV used to estimate CNVs. ATAC-seq performed with Transposase-Accessible Chromatin library prep, sequenced on Novaseq 6000, aligned with Bowtie2, peaks called with MACS2. Cell lines (22Rv1, RWPE-1) cultured per ATCC. Western Blot used antibodies against SOX9, KLF5, CTNNB1 etc. ChIP-PCR and ChIP-Seq validated CTNNB1 as a target of SOX9/KLF5. Flow cytometry analyzed CD8+CXCR6+ T cells and Tregs. IHC and IF staining used antibodies against AR, NKX3-1, KLK12, CD86, CD68, FAP, FOXP3 etc. Statistical analysis used GraphPad Prism 9.0 with unpaired t-tests or Mann-Whitney U-test.
